entry.title: My favourite photos
entry.content: Check out here for some of my favourite photos! It is based on alexivkin’s plugin which is great stuff.
entry.summary: Check out here for some of my favourite photos! It is based on alexivkin’s plugin which is great stuff.

entry.title: Here comes the sun
entry.content:
A few years back a family friend, Guy, asked for some help setting up the software on his home-made solar panel setup. He wanted an accessible way to edit certain necessary parameters (latitude/longitude, angle to return to at night etc.), so I threw together this Windows app.

These parameters were hidden in some big incomprehensible XML file, which one needs to access via FTP. My app was built in C#/.NET using the WPF (Windows Presentation Foundation) framework. It connects with Google Maps to easily set the lat./long. by using their magic search.

I’m quite impressed how the entire system has been running for nearly 5 years now (the app only came to mind recently as the Google Maps API I used was being deprecated, so I just fixed this); these panels which track the sun are significantly more effective than the fixed panel design.
entry.summary: A few years back a family friend, Guy, asked for some help setting up the software on his home-made solar panel setup. He wanted an accessible way to edit certain necessary parameters (latitude/longitude, angle to return to at night etc.), so I threw together this Windows app.

entry.title: UQ School of Maths & Physics Poster Day

link.title: UQ School of Maths & Physics Poster Day

entry.content: The UQ maths & physics poster day was fun! I showed some nice work that Pierre-Olivier, Søren & I did which is on arXiv; I tried to make my poster as accessible as possible.
entry.summary: The UQ maths & physics poster day was fun! I showed some nice work that Pierre-Olivier, Søren & I did which is on arXiv; I tried to make my poster as accessible as possible.

entry.title: Around-the-world conference trip
entry.content: I travelled to the International Conference on Monte Carlo Methods and Applications in Montreal, and the Foundations of Computational Mathematics conference in Barcelona. Both were amazing, though a month of travelling was slightly exhausting. Definitely check out the photos I took. A huge thanks to the UQ Graduate School for funding the trip through their Candidate Development Award, and to ACEMS for covering the conference fees!
entry.summary: I travelled to the International Conference on Monte Carlo Methods and Applications in Montreal, and the Foundations of Computational Mathematics conference in Barcelona. Both were amazing, though a month of travelling was slightly exhausting. Definitely check out the photos I took. A huge thanks to the UQ Graduate School for funding the trip through their Candidate Development Award, and to ACEMS for covering the conference fees!

entry.title: Made a new epic homepage for Søren

link.title: Made a new epic homepage for Søren

entry.content: Some days in the PhD you really look for anything to do that isn’t research.. I spent one of them making an updated homepage for my supervisor Søren Asmussen. The content is the same as the original site, but I just added some of his old photos to my new version.
entry.summary: Some days in the PhD you really look for anything to do that isn’t research.. I spent one of them making an updated homepage for my supervisor Søren Asmussen. The content is the same as the original site, but I just added some of his old photos to my new version.

entry.title: Greenland conference and hike
entry.content:
Ten mathematicians living off the land for a week in Greenland.. The photos we got were amazing; Leonardo did a great job of collating and editing them.

My mate Elliot was going to write a satirical/horror story based off this since it seemed so improbable (he assured me that, no matter which approach he took, the character version of me would get killed off for sure).

The reason we were all in Greenland was to attend the Applied Probability Symposium at Ilulissat which was a celebration for Søren Asmussen’s 70th birthday. I presented a talk at the conference — here are all the speakers in action.
entry.summary: Ten mathematicians living off the land for a week in Greenland.. The photos we got were amazing; Leonardo did a great job of collating and editing them.

entry.title: Cross-entropy example
entry.content:
Problem

Consider minimising the test function – named Rastrigin’s function – defined by

This is how it looks:

One cross-entropy approach could be as follows.

Formulation

State that

Consider a family of pdfs $\{ f(\cdot\,; v) : v \in \mathcal{V} \}$ on ${\mathbb{R}}^2$. We will construct a sequence of pdfs

which start with parameter $u=v_0$ and aim to converge on the zero-variance distribution whose mass is on the point $x^*$. In order to do this, we need to be able to solve the program:

where the ${ X_i } {\overset{\text{iid}}{\sim}} f(\cdot\,; v_{t-1})$.

Though this is simpler when $v_t$ is viewed as the maximum likelihood estimates (MLEs) of $\{ X_i \}$, which is known analytically for many distributions. In this case we’ve chosen $f$ to be of family of bivariate normal distributions, i.e.

The MLEs for $\mu_i$ and $\Sigma_i$ given some data $\{ X_i\}$ is simply the sample mean and the sample covariance matrix respectively.

Implementation

These are the samples evolving over time.

For those samples, these are the multivariate normal distributions which are fitted to the elite samples.

The objective function drops across iterations.

This is the same plot as above but on a log scale.
entry.summary: Cross-entropy, when used for optimization, is almost like a genetic algorithm. It creates a sequence of distributions which describe the location and certainty of the unknown minimum.

entry.title: Stochastic differential equations
entry.content:
Background: Brownian Motion

$\newcommand{\dif}{\,\mathrm{d}} $ A process $(B(t, \omega) : t \geq 0, \omega \in \Omega)$ is a standard Brownian Motion iff1:

  • $B(0) = 0$,

  • Independent increments, i.e $\forall~0 \leq t_1 \leq t_2 \leq t_3 \leq t_4$ then

  • $\forall~t,h\geq0 : B(t+h)-B(t) \sim \mathrm{N}(0,h)$,
  • $B(t)$ is almost-surely continuous w.r.t $t$.

Itô Integral

An Itô integral on $[0, T]$ is first defined for step processes. Say that the process $(X_n(t, \omega) : t \geq 0, \omega \in \Omega)$ is of the form

for $\omega \in \Omega$, $0 \leq t_1 \leq t_2 \leq {…}\leq t_n \leq T$ and ${\mathcal{F}}(t_i)$-measurable $A_i$. Then define the Itô integral to be

Next consider an $(X(t, \omega) : t \geq 0, \omega \in \Omega)$ process2. We will approximate the process by a sequence of step processes ${ X_n(t, \omega) : n \in {\mathbb{N}}, \omega \in \Omega }$. For all such $X(t, \omega)$ there exists an approximating sequence that, for all $t \geq 0$, satisfies

So define the general Itô integral to be the limit of integrals of step processes, i.e.

Itô Processes

For measurable processes $(a(t, \omega) : t \geq 0, \omega \in \Omega)$ and $(b(t, \omega) : t \geq 0, \omega \in \Omega)$ then an Itô process $(X(t, \omega) : t \geq 0, \omega \in \Omega)$ is written as

which is shorthand for

Diffusion Processes $\subset$ Itô Processes

A pair of deterministic functions $a(t, x)$ and $b(t, x)$ define a diffusion process $(X(t, \omega) : t \geq 0, \omega \in \Omega)$ by

which is shorthand for

Simulating Brownian Motion

At some fixed time $T$

As $B(T) \sim N(0, T)$ then simply generate (noting that MATLAB uses standard deviation instead of variance for many functions)

T = 5; % Time to sample the Brownian motion
B_T = normrnd(0, sqrt(T)); % Generate sample

On some finite mesh

Say for a mesh ${ t_1, {…}, t_n }$ then we know that $B(t_{i+1})-B_(t_i) \sim \mathrm{N}(0, t_{i+1}-t_i)$ so

Then to simulate

t = 0.1:0.1:1; % Setup time mesh (need not be an evenly spaced grid)
timeDiffs = [t(1), diff(t)]; % Calculate the time increments
incs = normrnd(0, timeDiffs); % Generate the BM increments
B = cumsum(incs); % Add them all up

or in one line

B = cumsum(normrnd(0, [t(1), diff(t)]));

Simulating diffusion processes by the Euler-Maruyama scheme

Consider some time mesh ${ t_1, {…}, t_n }$. Approximate $\dif t_i, \dif B(t_i)$ by

respectively and say

The Euler-Maruyama scheme approximates a diffusion process on a given time mesh by so

Say for example geometric Brownian motion is being simulated, i.e.

for $\mu, \sigma \in {\mathbb{R}}$.

% Constants
t = 0:0.1:10; % Time grid
mu = 0.5; % Drift
sigma = 0.3; % Diffusion
X_0 = 5; % Starting value (could be random)

% Output vector for realisation
X = zeros(1, numel(t)); 
X(1) = X_0; 

% Helper functions
deltaB = @(deltaT) normrnd(0, sqrt(deltaT));
deltaX = @(deltaT, x) mu*x*deltaT + sigma*x*deltaB(deltaT);

for i=2:numel(t)
deltaT = t(i)-t(i-1);
    X(i) = X(i-1) + deltaX(deltaT, X(i-1));
end

plot(t, X, '*-'); xlabel('t'); ylabel('X(t)'); title('Example GBM');

Or simply use MATLAB’s builtin SDE toolbox, the ‘gbm’ function

dt = 0.1; T = 10; nPeriods = numel(0:dt:T);
g = gbm(mu, sigma, 'StartState', X_0);
[X, t]=  g.simulate(nPeriods, 'DeltaTime', dt);
plot(t, X, '*-'); xlabel('t'); ylabel('X(t)'); title('Example GBM');

or more generally just using the ‘sde’ function

dt = 0.1; T = 10; nPeriods = numel(0:dt:T);
a = @(t,x) mu*x;
b = @(t,x)sigma*x;

d = sde(a, b, 'StartState', X_0);
[X, t]=  d.simulate(nPeriods, 'DeltaTime', dt);
plot(t, X, '*-'); xlabel('t'); ylabel('X(t)'); title('Example GBM');

Simulating multi-dimensional Itô processes by the Euler-Maruyama scheme

Equivalently the Euler-Maruyama method extends to multiple dimensions and works on general Itô processes. For example, for $({\boldsymbol{X}}(t, \omega) \in {\mathbb{R}}^2 : t \geq 0, \omega \in \Omega)$ then take

The algorithm to simulate this is given below.

% Time step, and some useful functions to calculate dBt & dXt
dt = 0.001;
dBt = @() normrnd(0, sqrt(dt), 2, 1);
dX = @(t, X) -X .* dt + [sin(t); cos(t)] .* dBt();
% Time horizon to plot over
t = 0:dt:10;
% Xt simulated values
X = zeros(2, numel(t));
% Perform Euler-Maruyama simulation
for i=2:numel(t)
X(:,i) = X(:,i-1) + dX(t(i), X(:,i-1));
end
% Plot the results
figure;
plot(X(1,:), X(2,:));

Milstein’s Method

Remember Taylor’s expansion about $(x_0, y_0)$ of a bivariate function

so therefore if $(x,y)$ is close to $(x_0,y_0)$ then an approximation for the derivative is

If we apply this to our SDE then

as

References: Higham, D. J. (2001). An algorithmic introduction to numerical simulation of stochastic differential equations. SIAM review, 43(3), 525-546. Available at: http://www.caam.rice.edu/~cox/stoch/dhigham.pdf

  1.  writing $B(t)$ for $B(t,\omega)$ ↩

  2.  which is progressively measurable and has ${\mathbb{E}}[\int_0^\infty X(s)^s \dif s] < \infty$ ↩
entry.summary: Notes for an informal lecture on simulating SDEs, considering basic diffusion processes with Matlab code.

entry.title: Variance reduction techniques
entry.content:
Antithetic Sampling

A typical CMC estimator for $l = {\mathbb{E}}[Z]$ is to take $N$ iid replications $\{Z_1, Z_2, \dots, Z_N\}$ and use their sample average, i.e.

This estimator will have variance

Another approach is to not sample $N$ random variables independently, but $N/2$ pairs of variables which have negative correlation. I.e. sample

where they are all of the same distribution and independent except for $Z_i$ with $Z^*_i$. The estimator is then

This has variance

Therefore

Say that random variables $X$ and $Y$ have the same marginal distributions ($F_X(z) = F_Y(z) = F(z)$) but are generated using $U_i {\overset{iid}{\sim}}U(0,1)$ by $X_i = F^{-1}(U_i)$ and $Y_i = F^{-1}(1-U_i)$ then ${\mathrm{Cov}}(X,Y)$ is minimal (see Theorem 5.3 in Ripley 2009, p. 130).\ Introduce dependence (specifically negative correlation) in your iterates to reduce variance.\

Control Variates

Say a simulation produces an output $Y$, and the same simulation also outputs another random variable $\tilde{Y}$ which is correlated with $Y$ and whose expectation $\tilde{l} = {\mathbb{E}}[\tilde{Y}]$ is known. The $\tilde{Y}$ is called a control variable for $Y$. Use the estimator

This will have variance

Note that:

So therefore

To find the $\alpha$ which minimises the variance then look for stationary points

So the minimum variance achieved by using control variates is

For each simulation find another variable – the control variable – that is highly correlated (positively or negatively) with the variable of interest which has known expectation (and preferably small variance).

Stratification

Stratified sampling uses the form $l = {\mathbb{E}}[{\mathbb{E}}[Y|Z]]$ and calculates the ${\mathbb{E}}[Y|Z]$ by partitioning the state space $\Omega$ into ${Z=i}, i=1, \dots, m$, the sections called strata. The general equation utilised is the law of total expectation, i.e.

Say that ${\mathbb{P}}(Z=i)=p_i$ are known constants, then for each strata choose $N_i$ (where $\sum_i N_i = N$) as the number to sample from that strata, and use the estimator:

Note that we are sampling from the conditional distributions of $Y$ given $Z=i$. The variance of this estimator is

So say ${\mathrm{Var}}[Y_{j \mid Z=i}] = \sigma^2_i$ then

In order to minimise the variance then select the $N_i$ by the rule

However it is rare that the variance of each strata is known so a pilot run can be used to estimate $\sigma_i$ using the sample variance. If using $N_i$ selected optimally as above, then the best variance of the estimator is then

Look for different “classes” in your state space (i.e strata) that partition the space, and have small variance, so that you can sample more effectively the noisy parts of the state space and infrequently sample the less noisy parts. See Ripley (2009, Chapter 5) for a detailed look at stratified sampling in statistics/survey theory.

References:

  • Cochran, W. G. (2007). Sampling techniques. John Wiley & Sons.
  • Ripley, B. D. (2009). Stochastic simulation (Vol. 316). John Wiley & Sons.
entry.summary: Notes made for an informal lecture on the Monte Carlo tricks available for variance reduction. This was made for a reading course when we worked through Søren's Monte Carlo book.

entry.title: The Pleasures of Life
entry.content:
Midway through honours, I stumbled across a gem of a book in an underground second-hand book store in Melbourne. Written by a long-dead English gentleman, The Pleasures of Life simply lists all the enjoyable aspects of being alive with heavy use of quotations from the canon. From the preface, the author explains:

“Having been myself when young rather prone to suffer from low spirits, I have at several of these gatherings taken the opportunity of dwelling on the privileges and blessings we enjoy, and I reprint here the substance of some of these addresses (omitting what was special to the circumstances of each case, and freely making any alterations and additions which have since occurred to me), hoping that the thoughts and quotations in which I have myself found most comfort may perhaps be of use to others also.”

I gifted my copy of the book to a close friend, but then couldn’t find another copy to replace it, so re-typeset the text found on Project Gutenberg in an old-timey font as close as possible to my original copy. It took a non-trivial amount of time, I remember the abundance of footnotes were the most difficult to replicate; the A5-sized version and the A4-sized version are here. The pièce de résistance was Rob Salomone’s photoshopping of an ancient portrait of the author into the sketch above.

Rob couldn’t help but give me the same treatment (below), and he called me Sir Patrick Laubbøck for the next few months ha.
entry.summary: Midway through honours, I stumbled across a gem of a book in an underground second-hand book store in Melbourne. Written by a long-dead English gentleman, The Pleasures of Life simply lists all the enjoyable aspects of being alive with heavy use of quotations from the canon. From the preface, the author explains:
